question.text: Scenario: Dr. Arun Patel, aged 50, is a senior physician earning Rs. 30 lakh per annum from his hospital practice. His wife Mrs. Shalini runs a small boutique earning Rs. 5 lakh per annum. They have one son, aged 22, who is completing his MBA and will be financially independent in 1 year. Dr. Patel wants to retire at 58. Their current financial position: Equity portfolio Rs. 45 lakh, Debt mutual funds Rs. 20 lakh, PPF Rs. 16 lakh (matures in 3 years), FDs Rs. 12 lakh, PF balance Rs. 25 lakh, Gold Rs. 10 lakh, Residential property (self-occupied) Rs. 1.2 crore. Liabilities: Home loan Rs. 15 lakh (EMI Rs. 22,000, 7 years remaining at 8%), no other loans. Monthly expenses: Rs. 1,80,000 (including EMI). Post-retirement, they estimate needing Rs. 1,20,000/month in today's terms. They want a retirement corpus that lasts 25 years post-retirement. Inflation is 6% p.a. Expected returns: equity 12%, debt 7%. Question 2 of 5: Dr. Patel's current net worth (excluding self-occupied property but including gold) is:

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Total assets (excluding self-occupied property) = 45 + 20 + 16 + 12 + 25 + 10 = Rs. 1,28,00,000. Liabilities = Rs. 15,00,000 (home loan). Net worth = 1,28,00,000 - 15,00,000 = Rs. 1,13,00,000. **The Trap:** Rs. 2,33,00,000 includes the self-occupied property. For retirement planning, exclude the home you live in. **Cross-Exam Context:** Net worth calculation with conservative asset estimation is core X-A (Ch 3). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Net worth of Rs. 1.13 crore at 50 is reasonable but needs aggressive growth over 8 years to fund a 25-year retirement.
